perturbation.original_text: "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line).
perturbation.changed_text: "Materials" means written information (limited to user manuals) regarding the Software and exclusively made available by Cisco on CD-Rom.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Documentation' broadly. The modified text replaces 'Documentation' with 'Materials' and provides a narrower definition, limiting it to user manuals available only on CD-Rom. This creates an in-text contradiction because the agreement later refers to 'Documentation' without clarifying if it means the broad or narrow definition. This contradiction will likely cause confusion over the scope of support materials that Cisco is obligated to provide, especially since it is referenced later on in the document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement and the license granted herein shall remain effective until terminated. Customer may terminate this Agreement and the license at any time by destroying all copies of Software including any Documentation. Customer's rights under this Agreement will terminate immediately without notice from Cisco if Customer fails to comply with any provision of this Agreement. Upon termination, Customer shall destroy all copies of Software and Documentation in its possession or control.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Documentation' broadly, while the changed text narrows the definition to 'Materials', limited to user manuals on CD-Rom. The contradicted text refers to 'Documentation' in the context of terminating the agreement and destroying all copies, implying a broader range of materials. By narrowing the definition in the modified text, it creates an in-text contradiction because it's unclear if the customer needs to destroy only the user manuals on CD-Rom or all forms of documentation as originally intended. This inconsistency regarding the scope of 'Documentation' directly impacts the enforcement of the termination clause, making the customer's obligation ambiguous and leading to a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: General Limitations. Except as otherwise expressly provided under this Agreement, Customer shall have no right, and Customer specifically agrees not to: (i) transfer, assign or sublicense its license rights to any other person or entity, or use the Software on unauthorized or secondhand Cisco equipment, and Customer acknowledges that any attempted transfer, assignment, sublicense or use shall be void; (ii) make error corrections to or otherwise modify or adapt the Software or create derivative works based upon the Software, or permit third parties to do the same; (iii) decompile, decrypt, reverse engineer, disassemble or otherwise reduce the Software to human- readable form; or (iv) use or permit the Software to be used to perform services for third parties without the express written authorization of Cisco.
perturbation.changed_text: General Limitations. Except as otherwise expressly provided under this Agreement, Customer shall have no right, and Customer specifically agrees not to: (i) transfer, assign or sublicense its license rights to any other person or entity, or use the Software on unauthorized or secondhand Cisco equipment, and Customer acknowledges that any attempted transfer, assignment, sublicense or use shall be void; (ii) alter the software or create derivative works based upon the Software, or permit third parties to do the same; (iii) decompile, decrypt, reverse engineer, disassemble or otherwise reduce the Software to human- readable form; or (iv) use or permit the Software to be used to perform services for third parties without the express written authorization of Cisco.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits 'error corrections' to the software. The modified text removes the explicit prohibition of 'error corrections', only stating 'alter the software'. This introduces ambiguity. Is making an 'error correction' considered 'altering the software?' It is uncertain in the text, since no definition is provided. This contradiction is problematic because it's unclear whether the customer is allowed to fix errors they encounter, which affects their ability to maintain the software's functionality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Limited Warranty Cisco warrants that commencing from the date of shipment to Customer (but in case of resale by an authorized Cisco reseller, commencing not more than ninety (90) days after original shipment by Cisco), and continuing for a period of the longer of (a) ninety (90) days or (b) the software warranty period (if any) set forth in the warranty card accompanying the Product (if any): (a) the media on which the Software is furnished will be free of defects in materials and workmanship under normal use; and (b) the Software substantially conforms to its published specifications. The date of shipment of a Product by Cisco is set forth on the packaging material in which the Product is shipped. Except for the foregoing, the Software is provided AS IS. This limited warranty extends only to the Customer who is the original licensee. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this limited warranty will be, at Cisco's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco. In no event does Cisco warrant that the Software is error free or that Customer will be able to operate the Software without problems or interruptions. In addition, due to the continual development of new techniques for intruding upon and attacking networks, Cisco does not warrant that the Software or any equipment, system or network on which the Software is used will be free of vulnerability to intrusion or attack.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited 'error corrections', which suggests a hands-off approach to the software. The changed text replaces this with a broader prohibition against 'altering the software'. This introduces ambiguity. The 'Limited Warranty' section outlines Cisco's responsibility for the software's functionality, stating that the 'Software substantially conforms to its published specifications' and offering 'repair, replacement, or refund' as a remedy. If the customer is not allowed to perform error corrections but the software doesn't conform to specifications, it creates a direct conflict. Is the customer entitled to repair the software themselves, or are they only entitled to have Cisco repair the software? The removal of the error correction prohibition and it's replacement to a broader prohibition makes the warranty unclear. As a result, the changed text creates an in-text contradiction. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Software, including technical data, may be subject to U.S. export control laws, including the U.S. Export Administration Act and its associated regulations, and may be subject to export or import regulations in other countries. Customer agrees to comply strictly with all such regulations and acknowledges that it has the responsibility to obtain licenses to export, re-export, or import Software.
perturbation.changed_text: Software, including technical data, may be subject to U.S. export control laws, including the U.S. Export Administration Act and its associated regulations. Customer agrees to comply strictly with all such regulations and acknowledges that it has the responsibility to obtain permits to export, re-export, or import Software.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses the term 'licenses' regarding compliance with export laws. The modified text replaces 'licenses' with 'permits' in the same sentence. While licenses and permits are often used interchangeably in common language, in legal and governmental contexts, they can have distinct meanings and processes associated with them. Because they are used interchangeably in this text, there is now an in-text contradiction. It is unclear if the user has to follow the required process for obtaining a 'license' or 'permit,' and both terms should not be used without distinguishing them from each other.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Software, including technical data, may be subject to U.S. export control laws, including the U.S. Export Administration Act and its associated regulations. Customer agrees to comply strictly with all such regulations and acknowledges that it has the responsibility to obtain permits to export, re-export, or import Software.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the term 'licenses' with 'permits,' while the contradicted text only uses the term 'permits.' This creates an in-text contradiction because the terms are used interchangeably without clarification, despite potentially distinct legal meanings. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, which states that using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them leads to conflicting obligations. It is unclear whether the customer needs to follow the process for obtaining a 'license' or a 'permit' according to the contract, leading to uncertainty.
